title: Dynamically Enabled Robustness of Geometric Phases and Entanglement in the Nonlinear Jaynes-Cummings Model
authors: Ali Martin Zynda, Paula I. Villar, Fernando C. Lombardo
abstract: Robustness in dissipative light-matter systems has recently been associated with resonance conditions or geodesic evolution. We show that, in the nonlinear Jaynes-Cummings model, these conditions are necessary but not sufficient. Using a Kerr-type extension together with a Lindblad description of cavity losses and atomic decoherence, we identify a dynamically enabled mechanism in which the stability of geometric phases and entanglement is governed by the alignment between coherent and dissipative trajectories in Hilbert space. Our results reveal that environmental action does not merely suppress quantum features, but reshapes the geometry of state-space evolution: protection emerges only when dissipation preserves the structure of the underlying unitary dynamics. This establishes a general geometric criterion for decoherence resilience in nonlinear light-matter systems and provides guiding principles for engineering protected evolution in open quantum platforms.
summary: This paper demonstrates that in nonlinear light-matter systems, protecting quantum features like geometric phases requires more than just resonance; it requires the dissipation to align with the system's unitary rotation. By showing that environmental action can reshape state-space geometry, the study offers new principles for engineering robust evolution in noisy quantum technologies.
main_problem: Investigating whether standard conditions like resonance or geodesic evolution are sufficient to protect geometric phases and entanglement from environmental decoherence in nonlinear light-matter systems.
main_result: The authors find that resonance and geodesic evolution are necessary but not sufficient for robustness; true protection requires a 'dynamically enabled' alignment where dissipation preserves the plane of the unitary trajectory.
method: Analytical diagonalization of the Hamiltonian in the low-excitation regime combined with numerical analysis of Lindblad master equation dynamics.
model_or_system: A nonlinear Jaynes-Cummings model featuring Kerr-type nonlinearity, describing a two-level atom coupled to a quantized bosonic mode subject to cavity loss, atomic relaxation, and dephasing.
key_observables: Geometric phase (GP), Negativity (entanglement), and population dynamics (Rabi oscillations).
important_parameters: Detuning (Delta), Kerr nonlinearity (chi), coupling strength (g), and dissipation rates (gamma, Gamma, Gamma_z).
main_assumptions: Zero-temperature environment, validity of the Rotating Wave Approximation (RWA), and restriction to the low-excitation regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares Negativity evolution in closed vs. open systems; Figure 2 shows Negativity for various nonlinearity values; Figure 3 quantifies GP sensitivity to initial conditions; Figure 4 shows GP deviation vs. detuning; Figure 5 illustrates the alignment of trajectories on the Bloch sphere for on-resonance vs. off-resonance cases.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the nonlinear JCM and dissipation model, defines the geometric phase and entanglement metrics, analyzes the necessity of geodesic paths, demonstrates the insufficiency of resonance alone, and concludes with a general geometric criterion for robustness.
why_interesting: It provides a new geometric principle for engineering decoherence-resilient quantum states by focusing on the alignment of coherent and dissipative trajectories, which is highly relevant for designing stable open quantum platforms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Negativity dynamics in time in resonance condition ∆= χ for different initial conditions for (a) closed systems and (b) open system evolution.

FIG. 2: Negativity dynamics in time for different values of ∆and χ = 0. The initial condition is perpendicular to de direction of the Hamiltonian; for (a) closed systems and (b) open system evolution. Changing χ only shifts this figure so that the symetry axis corresponds to resonance ∆= χ.
FIG. 4: Difference of GP between closed and open system after a time τ while varying ∆(off-resonance) but picking θ0 so that the initial condition is perpendicular to the Hamiltonian direction.

FIG. 3: Difference of GP between closed and open system after a time τ when varying initial condition direction θ0 while maintaining resonance ∆= χ so that the Hamiltonian represents rotation around the x-axis of the Bloch sphere.

FIG. 5: Trajectories on Bloch sphere for perpendicular initial conditions. The black arrow represents the Hamiltonian (i.e. the rotation axis) and the green arrow the initial condition. In black is the unitary trajectory, and in light-blue the projection of the density matrix in this space for a Lindbladian dynamic. Finally, in blue, is the eigenvector |ψ+(t)⟩which accumulates GP in the later case. Here, |0⟩≡|e0⟩and |1⟩≡|g1⟩.
